name: Yashovijaya
dates_notes: Dated by his own datable works (Saṃvat dates in colophons), by Vijayadevasūri's records of his Tapā-Gaccha investiture, and by his documented residence and study at Vārāṇasī under Brahmanical paṇḍitas in the 1640s; the dates 1624–1688 CE are settled in modern Jaina-historical scholarship (Tripāṭhī, Cort).

dates_evidence.description: Saṃvat colophon-dates in his own works fix the chronology.
Tapā-Gaccha records of his initiation and investiture as Upādhyāya.

lineage_polemical.note: Yaśovijaya studied Navya-Nyāya at Vārāṇasī in the technical idiom Gaṅgeśa established (Tattva-Cintāmaṇi) and engages it directly in his Jaina-Tarka-Bhāṣā and Nyāya-Loka; the Jaina-Navya-Nyāya synthesis he produces is a sustained refutation of the Naiyāyika positive theses (paramāṇu-cosmology, jāti-realism, the four-pramāṇa scheme) using the Naiyāyikas' own technical apparatus.

Raghunātha Śiromaṇi's Padārtha-Tattva-Nirūpaṇa reduces the Vaiśeṣika categories from seven to three, and Yaśovijaya engages this reduction; the Jaina-Tarka-Bhāṣā discusses the Padārtha-Tattva-Nirūpaṇa's reductions in light of the Jaina dravya-paryāya analysis, and the methodological convergence on category-reduction is itself a significant cross-tradition moment.

Appayya's late Vedāntic polemical works engage the Jaina position in the form Yaśovijaya systematises through Navya-Nyāya idiom; the engagement is indirect (Appayya does not cite Yaśovijaya by name) but the seventeenth-century Vedāntic Navya-Nyāya commentaries take the developed Jaina-Navya-Nyāya position as their target.

core_thesis: Yaśovijaya Upādhyāya is the major early-modern Śvetāmbara logician — the Jaina figure who restates the school's positions in Navya-Nyāya technical idiom. Trained at Vārāṇasī under Brahmanical paṇḍitas in the 1640s, he returns to the Tapā-Gaccha as the school's premier dialectician and writes a body of work covering Jaina pramāṇa-theory, *anekānta-vāda*, the seven-fold *bhaṅga*-predication, the *naya*-doctrine, and Jaina yoga-praxis — all reformulated in the technical vocabulary of *vyāpti*, *avacchedaka*, *paryāpti*, and *prakāra* that Gaṅgeśa's *Tattva-Cintāmaṇi* had established as the lingua franca of post-fourteenth-century Indian dialectic. The *Jaina-Tarka-Bhāṣā* states the Jaina theory of *pramāṇa* in Navya-Nyāya idiom; the *Nyāya-Loka* (also called *Nyāya-Khaṇḍa-Khādya*) is the most ambitious dialectical work of the corpus, defending Jaina positions on universals, particulars, the substance-modal account of reality, and the seven-fold predication against Naiyāyika opponents in the Naiyāyikas' own grammar. The *Adhyātma-Sāra* is the principal late-medieval Śvetāmbara text on the inner life — drawing Hemacandra's *Yoga-Śāstra* tradition together with the Adhyātma movement's emphasis on the *jīva*'s essential nature. Yaśovijaya also composed an extensive sub-commentary on Hemacandra's *Pramāṇa-Mīmāṃsā*. The strategic significance: by the seventeenth century, dialectical engagement with Brahmanical opponents required the Navya-Nyāya idiom, and Yaśovijaya is the figure who brings the Jaina position to the formal register required to engage seventeenth-century Vedāntic Navya-Nyāya commentators on their own ground.

engaged_works.title: Jaina-Tarka-Bhasha
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed in Yaśovijaya's mature period after his Vārāṇasī training; the title deliberately echoes Keśava Miśra's Tarka-Bhāṣā (the standard Navya-Nyāya introductory text) and Dharmarāja's Vedānta-Paribhāṣā.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely ascribed to Yaśovijaya by his own colophon and by uniform Tapā-Gaccha tradition.

ascription_evidence.description: Cort (Jains in the World); Tripāṭhī (modern editions of the Yaśovijaya corpus).

engaged_works.summary: A compact treatise on Jaina pramāṇa-theory and dialectical apparatus, deliberately modelled on Keśava Miśra's Navya-Nyāya *Tarka-Bhāṣā* and on Dharmarāja's Advaita *Vedānta-Paribhāṣā*. Yaśovijaya gives the Jaina theory of *pramāṇa* — *pratyakṣa* (with the *sāṃvyavahārika* / *mukhya* distinction) and *parokṣa* (with its five-fold subdivision) — in Navya-Nyāya technical vocabulary: *vyāpti*, *paryāpti*, *avacchedaka*, *prakāra* are deployed to articulate the Jaina positions on inferential structure and the relation between cognition and its object. The seven-fold *bhaṅga*-predication is presented in formal grammar showing how each *bhaṅga* is conditioned by an explicit *avacchedaka*; the *naya*-doctrine is articulated as a theory of standpoint-conditioned predication compatible with classical *anumāna*-theory. The work is the standard introductory text in which Jaina *pramāṇa*-theory is taught in Navya-Nyāya register, and the form in which the Tapā-Gaccha curriculum receives it.

engaged_works.title: Nyaya-Loka
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: The most ambitious dialectical work of Yaśovijaya's mature period.

ascription_evidence.description: Standard ascription in the Yaśovijaya canon.

engaged_works.summary: An extensive dialectical engagement with Naiyāyika positions on *jāti*-realism, *paramāṇu*-cosmology, the four-pramāṇa scheme, and the *Īśvara*-as-creator argument, conducted in the technical idiom of post-Raghunātha Navya-Nyāya. Yaśovijaya defends the Jaina substance-modal (*dravya-paryāya*) account of reality against the Naiyāyika substance-quality-particularity (*dravya-guṇa-viśeṣa*) ontology, arguing that the *paryāya*-modal aspect of every existent makes the seven-fold *bhaṅga*-predication unavoidable and that the Naiyāyikas' commitment to absolute *jāti* obscures it. The Naiyāyika five-membered inference is reformulated to allow the Jaina seven-fold predication to be tracked through the inferential structure. The work's title (*Khaṇḍa-Khādya*, 'a feast of refutations') announces its polemical character and is the most substantial seventeenth-century Jaina answer to Brahmanical Navya-Nyāya.

engaged_works.title: Adhyatma-Sara
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: The principal late-medieval Śvetāmbara text on the inner life.

ascription_evidence.description: Standard ascription; the work is canonical in the Śvetāmbara Adhyātma tradition.

engaged_works.summary: Twenty-one *adhikāras* on the inner life of the *jīva*: *adhyātma-svarūpa* (the nature of inner life), *dambha-tyāga* (the renunciation of pretence), *bhava-svarūpa* (the structure of saṃsāra), *vairāgya* (dispassion), *yoga* (the disciplines of *gupti*, *samiti*, *cāritra*), *dhyāna* (the four kinds of *dhyāna* — *ārta*, *raudra*, *dharma*, *śukla*), *anubhava* (the *jīva*'s direct experience of its own essential nature), and *mokṣa*. The work draws together Hemacandra's *Yoga-Śāstra* tradition and the Adhyātma movement's emphasis on the *jīva*'s essential nature as *cetana* and *jñāna-svabhāva* — the Kundakunda-derived tradition that the Śvetāmbara Adhyātma movement had absorbed from the Digambara Samaya-Sāra commentarial reception. Yaśovijaya tracks the Adhyātma reading carefully to avoid the Digambara *niścaya-naya* over-emphasis that the Tapā-Gaccha had been wary of, balancing it with explicit attention to the *vyavahāra-naya*'s role in praxis.

engaged_works.title: Pramana-Mimamsa Commentary
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely ascribed to Yaśovijaya by Tapā-Gaccha tradition.

ascription_evidence.description: Standard ascription in the Yaśovijaya canon.

engaged_works.summary: An extensive Sanskrit prose sub-commentary on Hemacandra's *Pramāṇa-Mīmāṃsā* — Hemacandra's twelfth-century systematic Jaina pramāṇa-treatise. Yaśovijaya unfolds Hemacandra's compressed *sūtras* and the auto-commentary in Navya-Nyāya idiom, integrating the Navya-Nyāya restatement of Jaina *pramāṇa*-theory he had developed in the *Jaina-Tarka-Bhāṣā* and *Nyāya-Loka*. The work is the form in which the Tapā-Gaccha curriculum receives the Hemacandra-Yaśovijaya pramāṇa-theory and is the principal seventeenth-century Śvetāmbara work for studying Jaina epistemology in the standard early-modern Sanskrit dialectical register.

reception_notes: Yaśovijaya is the figure through whom the Jaina position enters the seventeenth-century Sanskrit dialectical mainstream on the standard early-modern terms — Navya-Nyāya idiom, formal vyāpti-analysis, and the technical apparatus of the post-Raghunātha period. He is engaged (indirectly, by school) by the late Vedāntic Navya-Nyāya commentators (Madhusūdana's later commentators; Appayya's school); his works become the standard Tapā-Gaccha curriculum for advanced dialectics. Modern Jaina-historical scholarship (Tripāṭhī, Cort, Dundas) treats him as the school's most important early-modern dialectician and the figure who keeps Jaina logic in conversation with Brahmanical Navya-Nyāya into the colonial period.
